title: Sandhu Motor Finance Pvt Ltd vs Sunny on 15 January, 2024
body: Sandhu Motor Finance Pvt Ltd vs Sunny on 15 January, 2024 IN THE COURT OF SH. SOMITRA KUMAR, ADDITIONAL DISTRICT JUDGE - 06 (EAST DISTRICT), KARKARDOOMA COURTS, DELHI. CNR NO.:- DLET010087612023 RCA DJ NO.:- 33/2023 IN THE MATTER OF :- Sandhu Motors Finance Pvt. Ltd. At R/o 2-A/58, Geeta Colony, Delhi-110031, Through its Authorized Representative ....Appellant VERSUS 1. Sh. Sunny S/o Sh. Madan Lal, R/o 446, Jheel Khuranja, Krishna Nagar, Delhi-110051. 2. Sh. Rajinder S/o Sh. Gurbachan Singh, R/o 2A/78, Geeta Colony, Delhi-110031 ...Respondents Date of institution of the appeal : 05.07.2023 Date on which Judgment was reserved : 08.12.2023 Date of Decision : 15.01.2024 APPEAL UNDER SECTION 96 CIVIL PROCEDURE CODE AGAINST THE JUDGMENT/DECREE DATED 17.05.2023 PASSED BY LD. SENIOR CIVIL JUDGE, EAST, KARKARDOOMA COURTS, DELHI. JUDGMENT 1. The present appeal has been preferred under Section 96 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (hereinafter referred to as " CPC ") against the judgment/decree dated 17.05.2023 passed by Ld. Senior Civil Judge, Sh. Manoj Kumar, East, Karkardooma RCA No.33/23 Sandhu Motors Finance Pvt. Ltd. Vs. Sunny & Ors. Page No. 1 of 13 Courts, in Civil Suit No. 891/2022 whereby the suit of the appel- lant under Order XXXVII of CPC was decreed, however, in the said judgment, Ld. Trial Court declined the relief of the appellant qua the pendent-lite and future interest. Therefore, the present ap- peal seeks to set aside the judgment/decree dated 17.05.2023 of the Ld. Trial Court to the extent of non-grant of pendent-lite and future interest at the contractual rate of interest@36% per annum and prays for the grant of the contractual rate of interest@36% per annum for pendent-lite and future interest till the date of real- ization in favour of the appellant and against the respondents herein. 2. Succinctly stated, the necessary facts for adjudication of the present appeal, are as under:- i. That the appellant, a Non-Banking Finance Company duly incorporated under the Indian Companies Act and and registered with RBI, extended a personal loan of Rs. 30,000/- carrying interest @ 36% p.a. on 24.12.2019 for 24 months to respondent No. 1 with re- spondent No.1 as a borrower and respondent No.2 as guarantor vide loan agreement dated 24.12.2019 on the terms and conditions, stipulated therein. ii. The aforesaid loan was for a period of 24 months and in the discharge of the above-said loan, respondent No. 1 issued a post-dated cheque bearing no. 399742 dated 24.12.2021, for a sum of Rs. 51,600/- drawn on Canara Bank, Gandhi Nagar, Delhi-110031 in favour of the ap- pellant, which amount was inclusive of interest amount. However, the said cheque issued by respondent no.1 RCA No.33/23 Sandhu Motors Finance Pvt. Ltd. Vs. Sunny & Ors. Page No. 2 of 13 was returned unpaid with the remarks "FUNDS IN- SUFFICIENT" vide bank returning memo dated 27.12.2021. iii. A demand notice dated 01.02.2022 was issued to re- spondent no.1, however, respondent no. 1 failed to pay the due amount, whereafter, the appellant filed a com- plaint under section 138 of Negotiable Instrument Act, 1881 against respondent no.1, which is pending adjudi- cation before the Court of concerned Ld. Metropolitan Magistrate, Karkardooma Courts, Delhi. iv. That respondent no.2 being the guarantor to the above- said loan is also jointly and severally liable to pay the abovesaid loan amount. The appellant got issued one notice dated 08.03.2022, thereby requiring respondent no. 2 to pay the due amount, however, respondent No.2 did not pay the outstanding amount to the appellant. v. That as the respondents failed and neglected to pay the outstanding amount, therefore, the appellant herein filed a suit under Order XXXVII of CPC bearing no. CS 891/2022 on 19.10.2022 titled as Sandhu Motor Finance Pvt. Ltd. Vs. Sunny & Ors before Ld. Senior Civil Judge, East, Karkardooma Courts seeking recov- ery of 60,180/- along with interest @ 36% p.a. vi. That the summons of the suit were served upon the re- spondents, however, on account of the failure of re- spondents to file application for appearance as well as apply for leave to defend, vide judgement and decree dated 17.05.2023, the Ld. Trial Court decreed the suit RCA No.33/23 Sandhu Motors Finance Pvt. Ltd. Vs. Sunny & Ors. Page No. 3 of 13 of the appellant for a sum of Rs.60,180/-, however, de- clined the prayer of the appellant herein to pendente lite and future interest stating that the appellant herein has already claimed interest at an exorbitant rate @ 36% per annum w.e.f. 24.12.2019 till 10.10.2022 and the awarded amount of Rs. 60,180/- is inclusive of said in- terest amount. 3. Hence, feeling aggrieved, the appellant filed the present appeal challenging judgment/decree dated 17.05.2023 on the grounds, inter alia, that the impugned judgment passed by the Ld. Trial Court is against the provisions of order 37 CPC ; the Ld. Trial Court has failed to appreciate that the appellant has claimed the interest at the agreed rate and as per the terms of the loan agreement entered between the parties; the Ld. Trial Court has failed to appreciate that the loan was advanced to the appellant carrying interest @ 36% р.а., and as such the respondents are li- able to pay to the appellant interest @ 36% р.а.; the Ld. Trial Court has failed to appreciate that the terms of the agreement be- tween the parties, cannot be altered by the Ld. Trial Court and it can be altered by the parties only and not otherwise; the Ld. Trial Court has failed to appreciate that the amount of Rs.60,180/- as claimed by the appellant was inclusive of interest only qua the pre-suit interest and not thereafter; as per law, as respondents failed to file an appearance and further failed to apply for leave to defend, therefore the facts stated in the plaint are deemed to have been admitted by the respondents and as such the rate of in- terest stands admitted, therefore, there was no occasion for the Ld Trial Court to decline the interest in favour of the appellant, RCA No.33/23 Sandhu Motors Finance Pvt. Ltd. Vs. Sunny & Ors. Page No. 4 of 13 and the Ld. Trial Court has wrongly declined the pendente lite and future interest on non-existent grounds. 4. Respondent no. 2 filed a reply to the appeal denying the grounds of the appellant for challenging the impugned judgment/decree and contested the present appeal stating that the interest claimed by the appellant is exorbitant and is outside the bounds of law. 5. The Trial Court Record (TCR) was summoned and argu- ments have been heard as addressed by Sh. Sudhir Gupta, Ld. Counsel for appellant, and Sh. Jatin Malhotra, Ld. Counsel for respondent no. 2. Despite ample opportunities, neither anyone appeared on behalf of respondent no. 1 to address the argument nor any reply was filed. 6. During the hearing, Ld. Counsel for appellant placed re- liance on the judgment of Hon'ble Supreme Court of India in the matter of TVC Skyshop Ltd. vs. Reliance Communication and Infrastructure Ltd. (2013(11) SCC 754) and Hon'ble High Court of Delhi in DHL Worldwide Express vs. Associated Buying Ser- vices ((79(1999) DLT 168); Indian Bank vs. Cheese Wafers (In- dia) Pvt. Ltd. (Delhi High Court, date of decision 29.04.1998); The Right Address Limited vs. Growth Techno Projects Ltd. & Anr. (2009(6) R.A.J. 385(Del)) and Voltas Limited & Ors. Vs. Union of India & Ors. (2009(156) DLT 122). 7. Submissions made by Ld. Counsel for both the parties be- fore the Court have been considered and the record has been pe- rused carefully. RCA No.33/23 Sandhu Motors Finance Pvt. Ltd. Vs. Sunny & Ors. Page No. 5 of 13 8. In the present appeal, what arises for consideration is whether the Ld. Trial was justified in declining the prayer of the plaintiff for pendente-lite and future interest @36% per annum, and whether the appellant is entitled to pendente-lite and future interest at a contractual rate as per applicable law. 9. The relevant provision in the Code of Civil Procedure , 1908 governing the grant of interest rate is Section 34 of CPC, which is reproduced below. 34. Interest. (1) Where and in so far as a decree is for the payment of money, the Court may, in the decree, order interest at such rate as the Court deems reasonable to be paid on the principal sum adjudged, from the date of the suit to the date of the decree, in addition to any interest adjudged on such principal sum for any period prior to the institution of the suit,with further interest at such rate not exceeding six per cent per annum as the Court deems reasonable on such principal sum, from the date of the decree to the date of payment, or to such earlier date as the Court thinks fit : Provided that where the liability in relation to the sum so adjudged had arisen out of a commercial transaction, the rate of such further interest may exceed six per cent per annum, but shall not exceed the contractual rate of interest or where there is no contractual rate, the rate at which moneys are lent or advanced by nationalised banks in relation to commercial transactions. Explanation I.--In this Sub-section, "nationalised bank" means a corresponding new bank as defined in the Banking Companies (Acquisition and Transfer of Undertakings) Act, 1970 (5 of 1970). Explanation II.-- For the purposes of this section, a transaction is a commercial transaction, if it is connected with the industry, trade or business of the party incurring the liability. (2) Where such a decree is silent with respect to the payment of further interest on such principal sum from the date of the decree to the date of payment or other earlier date, the Court shall be deemed to have refused such interest, and a separate suit therefor shall not lie. RCA No.33/23 Sandhu Motors Finance Pvt. Ltd. Vs. Sunny & Ors. Page No. 6 of 13 10. The proviso to Section 34 of CPC provides that a Court may grant future interest at a rate more than six percent but less than contractual if the liability in relation to the principal sum adjudged had arisen out of a commercial transaction. 11. Explanation II to section 34 of CPC defines a commercial transaction as a transaction connected with the industry, trade or business of the party incurring the liability. 12. Only in the case of commercial transactions, does the proviso to Section 34 CPC, which talks about the contractual interest rate, can be pressed into service. In the case of non- commercial transactions, the main body of section 34 CPC is applicable. As averred in the plaint as well as in the memorandum of appeal by the appellant, the appellant extended a personal loan to respondent no. 1 and there is no averment in pleadings of appellant to the effect that the loan transaction was in connection with the industry, trade, or business of the borrower i.e. respondent no. 1. Therefore, proviso to section 34 of CPC does not have an application to the facts of the present matter and the grant of the interest in the present matter shall be governed by the main body of section 34 of CPC. 13. In view of the fact that the present matter does not relate to a commercial transaction, therefore, with all due respect to the decision cited by the appellant, the same shall not apply to the facts and circumstances of the present matter as all the citations relied upon by the appellant concern with commercial transactions. 14. As regards the application of section 34 of CPC to award interest, it is pertinent to refer to the decision of the Hon'ble RCA No.33/23 Sandhu Motors Finance Pvt. Ltd. Vs. Sunny & Ors. Page No. 7 of 13 Supreme Court of India in the matter Central Bank of India v. Ravindra , (2002) 1 SCC 367, wherein section 34 of CPC was dealt in detail. The relevant part of the aforesaid judgement of the Hon'ble Apex Court of India is reproduced hereinbelow: "Such principal sum" -- meaning of 40. Let us paraphrase the relevant part of Section 34(1) as under and then deal with the question posed before us: "Where and insofar as a decree is for the payment of money, the court may, in the decree, order interest at such rate as the court deems reasonable to be paid on the principal sum adjudged, from the date of the suit to the date of the decree, in addition to any interest adjudged on such principal sum for any period prior to the institution of the suit, with further interest at such rate not exceeding six per cent per annum as the court deems reasonable on such principal sum, from the date of the decree to the date of payment, or to such earlier date as the court thinks fit." (emphasis supplied) 41. A few points are clear from a bare reading of the provision. While decreeing a suit if the decree be for payment of money, the court would adjudge the principal sum on the date of the suit. The court may also be called upon to adjudge interest due and payable by the defendant to the plaintiff for the pre-suit period which interest would, on the findings arrived at and noted by us hereinabove, obviously be other than such interest as has already stood capitalised and having shed its character as interest, has acquired the colour of the principal and having stood amalgamated in the principal sum would be adjudged so. The principal sum adjudged would be the sum actually loaned plus the amount of interest on periodical rests which according to the contract between the parties or the established banking practice has stood capitalised. Interest pendente lite and future interest (i.e. interest post-decree not exceeding 6 per cent per annum) shall be awarded on such principal sum i.e. the principal sum adjudged on the date of the suit. It is well settled that the use of the word "may" in Section 34 confers a discretion on the court to award or not to award interest or to award interest at such rate as it deems fit. Such interest, so far as future interest is concerned may commence from the date of the decree and may be made to stop running either with payment or with such earlier date as the court thinks fit. Shortly RCA No.33/23 Sandhu Motors Finance Pvt. Ltd. Vs. Sunny & Ors. Page No. 8 of 13 hereinafter we propose to give an indication of the circumstances in which the court may decline award of interest or may award interest at a rate lesser than the permissible rate. (emphasis supplied) . . . 46. It was submitted from the borrowers' side that such an interpretation of Section 34 of the Civil Procedure Code as canvassed on behalf of the banks, if accepted, may result in anomalous situations emerging. To wit, it was pointed out that if the bank deliberately and unscrupulously delays the suit being filed, for such period of delay the bank would gain an advantage by continuing to charge interest at the contract rate and by capitalising the same. If the suit was filed promptly then the contract would cease to operate and the debtor would be relieved from the rigour of the contract and find solace under the operation of Section 34 of the Civil Procedure Code. True it is that once a suit is filed in the court, so far as Section 34 of the Civil Procedure Code is concerned, the relationship of parties ceases to be governed by contract between the parties and comes to be governed by Section 34 of the Civil Procedure Code. Still the submission has to be repelled for several reasons. Firstly, the bank can afford to wait or delay the filing of the suit only during the period of limitation which delay would not be illegitimate. Secondly, nothing prevents the debtor, even during the period of this delay, to pay or tender the amount of interest as and when it falls due and thereby prevent its capitalisation. Thirdly, the court is not powerless to deny the bank's claim for interest, if in the facts and circumstances of a given case the court is persuaded to hold that filing of the suit was delayed for the purpose of deliberately gaining an unfair advantage over adverse financial condition of the defendant. In such cases the pre-suit interest though claimed in accordance with the contract would be denied by the court on the ground of public policy and on the ground of the creditor having tried to gain an unfair advantage over the debtor by a deliberate inaction of himself; no one can take advantage of his own wrong. (emphasis supplied) . . . . RCA No.33/23 Sandhu Motors Finance Pvt. Ltd. Vs. Sunny & Ors. Page No. 9 of 13 58. Subject to the above we answer the reference in the following terms: (1) Subject to a binding stipulation contained in a voluntary contract between the parties and/or an established practice or usage interest on loans and advances may be charged on periodical rests and also capitalised on remaining unpaid. The principal sum actually advanced coupled with the interest on periodical rests so capitalised is capable of being adjudged as principal sum on the date of the suit. (2) The principal sum so adjudged is "such principal sum" within the meaning of Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 on which interest pendente lite and future interest i.e. post-decree interest, at such rate and for such period which the court may deem fit, may be awarded by the court." 15. Further, it has been laid down in paragraph 55(8) of the aforesaid judgement of the Hon'ble Supreme Court of India that "Award of interest pendente lite and post-decree is discretionary with the court as it is essentially governed by Section 34 CPC dehors the contract between the parties. In a given case if the court finds that in the principal sum adjudged on the date of the suit, the component of interest is disproportionate with the component of the principal sum actually advanced the court may exercise its discretion in awarding interest pendente lite and post-decree interest at a lower rate or may even decline awarding such interest. The discretion shall be exercised fairly, judiciously and for reasons and not in an arbitrary or fanciful manner." 16. As observed in paragraphs 46 of Central Bank of India (supra) once a suit is filed in the court, so far as Section 34 of the Civil Procedure Code is concerned, the relationship of parties ceases to be governed by the contract between the parties and comes to be governed by Section 34 of the Civil Procedure Code. RCA No.33/23 Sandhu Motors Finance Pvt. Ltd. Vs. Sunny & Ors. Page No. 10 of 13 17. Therefore, after filing the suit in the present matter, the grant of pendent lite and future interest would be governed by section 34 of CPC and not by the contractual rate of interest. Further, as discussed above, in the present matter, as per averment in the plaint, the loan facility extended was for a personal loan. There is not even a single averment in the plaint that the loan facility was extended in connection with the business of borrowers. Thus, the transaction in the present matter is not a commercial transaction, and hence, the pendente lite and future interest are to be awarded as per the provision contained in the main body of section 34 of CPC and the contractual rate of interest to determine the rate of pendente lite and future interest does not apply to the facts of the present matter. 18. As per the mandate of section 34 of CPC, a pendente lite interest at such rate the court deems reasonable and a future interest not exceeding 6 percent may be granted in the discretion of the court on the principal sum adjudged by the Court. 19. As regards the contention of the appellant that in view of fact that the present matter is in relation to a suit for recovery under Order XXXVII of CPC and the respondents failed to contest the suit, therefore the facts stated in the plaint including the rate of interest are deemed to have been admitted by the respondents, the Court does not find any force in the contention of the appellant. Even if the respondents failed to contest the suit of the appellant under Order XXXVII of CPC , the same does not take away the discretion conferred on the Court to grant pendente lite and future interest under Section 34 of CPC. Order XXXVII Rule 3(6)(b) of CPC entitles the plaintiff to judgment if the RCA No.33/23 Sandhu Motors Finance Pvt. Ltd. Vs. Sunny & Ors. Page No. 11 of 13 defendant fails to apply for leave to the defend within the prescribed time. However, said provision is subject to other provisions of the Code, and, said provision cannot in any way supersede the express provision of Section 34 of CPC. There is no basis for contention of the appellant either in statute or in any case law. 20. After the conclusion of the arguments, Ld. Counsel for the appellant filed many judgemetns on record. The first judgment is of Hon'ble Supreme Court of India in H.U.D.A. vs. Raj Singh Rana 2009(17) SCC 199. With all due respect, said judgement relates to the applicability of the Interest Act, 1978 to proceeding before a Court, and as per Section 5 of the Interest Act, 1978, nothing in the Interest Act, 1978 shall apply to Section 34 of CPC. In other words, Section 34 of CPC has an overriding effect over the Interest Act, 1978 . The second judgment is also of the Hon'ble Supreme Court of India in Syndicate Bank vs. R. Veeranna 200392) SCC 15. With all due respect, the said judgement does not deal with the issue of award of pendent lite and future interest in a suit of recovery of a loan amount. Rather the said judgment of the Hon'ble Supreme Court of India dealt with the issue of enhancement of rate of interest by the bank over and above initially agreed in the contract with the customer. The third judgment is of Hon'ble Karnataka High Court in Vijaya Bank vs. S. Bhathija & Anr. AIR 1994 KANT. 123. With all due respect , the said judgment in paragraph 18 relies upon the proviso of section 34 of CPC for charging of pendent lite and future interest. The proviso to Section 34 of CPC comes into play in case of commercial transactions, therefore, said judgment does not apply to the facts of the present case. RCA No.33/23 Sandhu Motors Finance Pvt. Ltd. Vs. Sunny & Ors. Page No. 12 of 13 21. In the light of aforesaid observations, the Court is not inclined to grant the prayer of the appellant for the grant of pendente lite and future interest @36 % per annum. However, in view of the provision in section 34 of CPC and in the interest of justice, the Court modifies the judgment and decree of the Ld. Senior Civil Judge, East, Karkardooma Court to the extent that appellant is awarded pendent lite @9% per annum and future interest @6% per annum, till the actual realization of the amount, on the sum of INR 60,180/-. The suit is accordingly decreed. 22. The appeal stands disposed of accordingly. 23. Copy of this judgment along with Trial Court Record be sent back to the Ld. Trial Court for information. 24. No order as to cost is made. 25. A decree-sheet be prepared accordingly after payment of the Court fees on appeal. 26. Appeal File be consigned to Record Room after due compliance. Pronounced in the open court on 15th January, 2024. (SOMITRA KUMAR) ADJ-06,EAST/KKD COURTS, Delhi/15.01.2024. RCA No.33/23 Sandhu Motors Finance Pvt. Ltd. Vs. Sunny & Ors. Page No. 13 of 13
